coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-combined ratiometric fluorescence probe for specifically and visibly detecting cephalexin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Ai-Yue
Wang, Xue-Qing
Mei, Yan-Zhen
Nie, Jun-Fang
Yang, Ya-Qiong
Dai, Chuan-Chao
coredata.dc:description: A smartphone-combined dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for specifically and visibly detecting cephalexin was first designed. In the probe, blue-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized and covered with a layer of silica spacer. Red-emitting fluorescent CdTe QDs (r-QDs) was grafted onto the silica nanospheres as an analytical probe. Then, the cephalexin antibody was covalent grafted to the ratio sensor to increase the selectivity. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (FL) of r-QDs and CDs was quenched with the increasing concentration of cephalexin. The detection method has good linear response in the range of 1–500 μM and the detection limit was 0.7 μM. Then portable device based on smartphone detection was constructed according to the color change under UV lamp. The detection image was obtained through the smartphone camera, and the color picker APP installed in the smartphone captured the RGB value of the image. In addition, this method was also used to determine the amount of cephalexin in milk samples with recovery of 94.1%-102.2%. These results showed that it was a portable, simple and visible method to detect cephalexin in food analysis and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Cephalexin
Smartphone
Portable device

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of antibody-modified ratiometric fluorescence probes (CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab)
Preparation of CdTe QDs and Ab modified CDs@SiO2 (CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab)
Design and synthesize of antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Characterization of the antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Optical propertied of the antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor for cephalexin detection
Portable device with smartphone as analyzer for cephalexin detection in milk

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A), r-QDs (B), CDs@SiO2 (C), CDs@SiO2-r-QDs (D), and CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (E).

(A) The FT-IR spectra of CDs (a), CDs@SiO2 (b), r-QDs (c), CDs@SiO2-r-QDs (d), CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (e). (B) The zeta potential of CDs (a), CDs@SiO2 (b), r-QDs (c), CDs@SiO2-r-QDs (d), CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (e). (C) XPS spectrum of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab nanoprobe. (D) Fluorescent spectra of CDs (a), CD@SiO2 (b), r-QDs (c) and CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (d), the inset of Fig. 2D was the photograph of nanoparticles in the 365 UV lamp.

(A) Responsiveness of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab to the concentration changes of cephalexin corresponding to the incubation time. (B) The fluorescence stability of the aqueous solutions of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab over time at 4 °C under refrigerator. (C) The temperature stability of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab. (D) The effects of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab. I494 nm was the fluorescence intensity at 494 nm, I608 nm was the fluorescence intensity at 608 nm. (I608/I494)0 and (I608/I494) were the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab system in the absence and presence of cephalexin, respectively.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab after adding the different concentration of cephalexin, the inset of figure (A) was the photography of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab after adding the different concentration of cephalexin, a-i: 0 μM, 1 μM, 5 μM, 25 μM, 50 μM, 150 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM, 500 μM. (B) Linear relationship between the concentration of cephalexin and the ratio of fluorescence quenching intensity of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab. (C) The fluorescence quenching of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab after their incubation with a solution of cephalexin (500 μM) or a solution of 10-folds of its analogue (amoxicillin, ampicillin, penicillin sodium, chloramphenicol and atenolol) and other substances that may be present in milk (including cysteine, glutamic acid and glucose, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cl-) (blue column) or a solution of cephalexin (500 μM) in the presence of 10-folds of its analogue and other substances (pink column). (D) UV–vis spectra of the solution of cephalexin (a) and CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (b) and fluorescent emission spectrum of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab solution (c).

(A) Scheme outline for smartphone-combined cephalexin detection in milk. (B) The Color picker APP installed in smartphone. (C) Images of sample solutions containing various concentrations of cephalexin under 365 UV lamp: 0 μM, 1 μM, 5 μM, 25 μM, 50 μM, 150 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM, 500 μM. (D) The liner relationship between the ratio of R/B and cephalexin concentrations.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescence probe preparation for cephalexin detection.
The performance of our optical sensing method compared with other previously reported cephalexin detection methods.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-combined ratiometric fluorescence probe for specifically and visibly detecting cephalexin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A smartphone-combined dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for specifically and visibly detecting cephalexin was first designed. In the probe, blue-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) was synthesized and covered with a layer of silica spacer. Red-emitting fluorescent CdTe QDs (r-QDs) was grafted onto the silica nanospheres as an analytical probe. Then, the cephalexin antibody was covalent grafted to the ratio sensor to increase the selectivity. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (FL) of r-QDs and CDs was quenched with the increasing concentration of cephalexin. The detection method has good linear response in the range of 1–500 μM and the detection limit was 0.7 μM. Then portable device based on smartphone detection was constructed according to the color change under UV lamp. The detection image was obtained through the smartphone camera, and the color picker APP installed in the smartphone captured the RGB value of the image. In addition, this method was also used to determine the amount of cephalexin in milk samples with recovery of 94.1%-102.2%. These results showed that it was a portable, simple and visible method to detect cephalexin in food analysis and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Cephalexin is the first-generation cephalosporin, which is white or milky yellow crystalline powder, and its solid state is more stable than its in aqueous solutions. It has a broad antibacterial spectrum, which effects on both Gram-positive bacteria and Gram-negative bacteria [1–3]. The effect of the antibacteria is stronger than that of the second and third generation cephalosporins. Cephalexin is widely used to treat diseases such as urethral infections, skin infections, respiratory infections and mastitis [4–6]. With the widespread use of cephalexin in animal husbandry, there are many residues in many animal products such as meat, eggs, and milk. The presence of these residues should be highly valued because of their potential carcinogenic properties, which may produce antibiotic resistance and people's allergic reactions [7,8]. Therefore, in order to protect people's safety, many institutions including the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA), World Health Organization (WHO) and European Union (EU) have set maximum residue limits (MRLs) for antibiotics in different food. For example, the MRLs of cephalexin are 100 μg·kg−1 for milk in EU [9]. Therefore, finding a simple, fast and sensitive method is urgent and important.

So far, many strategies have been proposed to detect cephalexin residues, such as electrochemistry [10,11], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [12], colloidal gold immunochromatography [13], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14] and high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [15]. Although chromatography is widely used as analytical method for detection, its operation is complex and requiring specialized personnel, expensive equipment and long analysis time. It cannot meet the development trend of many companies demanding the convenience and miniaturization of analytical instruments.

Semiconductor fluorescent nanocrystals, quantum dots (QDs) have gradually replaced traditional fluorescent materials due to their narrow, adjustable emission spectrum and long fluorescence lifetime [16–18]. They have widely used as biological and chemical sensing materials for the detection of various substances. Fluorescent enhancement or quenching was often adopted for detection of analyte. However, single emission fluorescent signals are easily interfered by environmental factors such as temperature and solvent polarity [19–21]. In order to eliminate background interference and improve detection sensitivity and accuracy, ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual emission fluorescent nanoparticles have received increasing attention [22,23]. The ratiometric fluorescent probe is designed by connecting two fluorophores with different emission wavelengths through covalent bonds, which can be obtained by combining the identification fluorescent substance and the reference fluorescent substance into nanoparticles [24,25]. Ratiometric fluorescence probe is ideal for a variety of sample analysis and biological imaging. As far as we know, this is the first application of smartphone-combined ratiometric fluorescent probe to detect cephalexin. Moreover, the ratiometric assay could produce a distinguished color change, which is more conducive for visual detection.

In our work, we established a colorimetric fluorescent sensing platform for high-sensitivity and visual detection of cephalexin. In short, blue fluorescent CDs were prepared and coated with silicon and amino group. Then red-emitting CdTe quantum dots were assemblied on the surface of silica-coated CDs (CDs@SiO2). At last, the synthetic probe was modified with cephalexin antibody (Ab). Once the probe was exposed to the environment of cephalexin, the ratio of FL will response with the increasing concentration of the cephalexin. And the fluorescence of the prepared nanoprobe will shift from red to blue, so that cephalexin can be detected visually and quantitatively with the aid of UV lamp. At the same time, the probe combined with the portable detection device of the smart phone color picker APP, could further verify the reliable detection of cephalexin in milk.

All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Lactose (Ling Feng Chemicals Co. Ltd., AR), Tris (Shanghai Shaoyuan Reagent Co. Ltd., 99%), ethyl alcohol (Sinopharm Chemicals, 95%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, Ling Feng Chemicals Co. Ltd., 99%), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, TCI Chemicals Co. Ltd., Shanghai, 98%), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, Ling Feng Chemicals Co. Ltd., 28%), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, Yuanye Chemicals Co. Ltd., 99%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd., 98%), cephalexin (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), amoxicillin (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), ampicillin (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), penicillin sodium (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥97%), chloramphenicol (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), atenolol (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), cysteine (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), glutamic acid (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), glucose (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd., ≥98%), cephalexin antibody (Ab) was purchased from Wuhan Cloud-Clone Corp. Red-emitting CdTe QDs were from Janus New-materials Co. Ltd.. Pure milk (Inner Mongolia Mengniu Dairy (Group) Co. Ltd.) was purchased from a local supermarket.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Vertex 70, Bruker, Germany). X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with an electron microscope operating (Jem-2100F, Jeol, Japan). TEM samples were prepared by dropping the samples onto carbon-coated copper grids and allowing the excess solvent to evaporate. The UV–vis adsorption spectra of the samples were measured on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The zeta potential was measured by Zetasizer Nano ZSE (Nano ZSE, Great Britain).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of antibody-modified ratiometric fluorescence probes (CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab)

ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized according to the previous report with some modification [26]. Briefly, 2.0 g of Tris and 0.125 g of lactose were dissolved in 40 mL of pure water. The mixed solution was hydrothermally refluxed at 100 °C for 24 h with stirring, and the color changed from colorless into yellow or even brown. After cooled down to room temperature, we neutralized the pH with hydrochloride acid, and store it at 4 °C until further use.

CDs@SiO2 was synthesized by the sol–gel method according to the previous method with some modifications [27]. Generally, 8.0 mL of CDs was dispersed in 40 mL of ethanol. Then 120 μL of TEOS, 40 μL of APTES and 200 μL of NH3·H2O were added, and stirred for 6 h. Finally, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 5 min, washed twice with ethanol and pure water, and dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe QDs and Ab modified CDs@SiO2 (CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab)
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab, the dry CDs@SiO2 powder was dissolved in pure water under ultrasonic conditions, 1 mL of CDs@SiO2 (1 mg·mL−1) was mixed with 4 mL of CdTe QDs, 1 mL of EDC/NHS (0.16 mg·mL−1) and stirred for 1.0 h. Then, 800 µL of Ab (8 μg/mL) and 1 mL of EDC/NHS (0.16 mg·mL−1) were dispersed into the above solution under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, the product was obtained by centrifugation and washed for several times with ethanol and then dried under vacuum. CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab were dispersed into pure water by ultra-sonication and stored at 4 °C.

For the detection of cephalexin in solution, 0.5 mL of cephalexin solutions with different concentrations (0 μM, 1 μM, 5 μM, 25 μM, 50 μM, 150 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM, 500 μM) were added to 0.5 mL of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab aqueous solution. Then the mixture was incubated for 1 h and observed the change of fluorescence. In order to obtain accurate experimental results, all experiments were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the slit widths of excitation and emission of 5 nm, and a photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V.

Several concentrations (0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 50 μM) of cephalexin were added to milk and homogenized the spiked sample. It was kept at room temperature for about 20 min to balance the cephalexin with the milk matrix before extraction. Then 1 mL of methanol was added to 500 μL of milk to minimize interference from the milk matrix. After the mixture was stirred for 10 min, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 6 min, and the precipitate was removed. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane, dried with nitrogen gas, and the volume was adjusted to 500 μL with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) for analysis [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesize of antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The process of constructing ratiometric fluorescence probe to detect cephalexin is shown in Scheme 1 . We used a mixed solution of lactose and Tris at 100 °C for 24 h to prepare strongly blue CDs. The synthesized CDs were then coated silica spacer with TEOS and ammonia, and then the surface of CDs@SiO2 was amino-functionalized with APTES. The purchased red-emitting CdTe quantum dots were synthesized in the aqueous phase, and the surface was modified with mercaptopropionic acid. The carboxyl modified CdTe QDs were grafted on the surface of amino-functional CD@SiO2. Finally, the cephalexin antibody was modified on the surface of the red CdTe QDs. On the one hand, the reference fluorescent CDs@SiO2 could increase the selectivity of sensor because it can eliminate background interference. Meanwhile, ratio sensor ensured their color change of probe. On the other hand, antibody-modification endows the probe with high specificity. On this basis, our sensor was suitable to ratiometric and visible detection of cephalexin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of CDs, CDs@SiO2, CdTe QDs, CDs@SiO2-r-QDs, and CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab were investigated by TEM (Fig. 1 ). From the Fig. 1a, CD was spherical with uniform size and good dispersibility, and its average particle size was about 2 nm. CdTe QDs maintained a similar spherical morphology with an average size of approximately 2.8 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c revealed that CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles were uniform sphere with a diameter of about 103 ± 3 nm. It can be seen from Fig. 1d that the diameter of CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles grafted with r-QDs does not change much, and they were uniformly dispersed nanospheres. After the cephalexin antibody was attached to the surface of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs, the size of the as-prepared particle was 104 ± 5 nm (Fig. 1e).

FT-IR was also used to investigate the functional group composition of the synthesized CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab. From the Fig. 2 A-a, the characteristic peak at 3385 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, proving the successful synthesis of hydroxyl-modified CD. The peak near 1058 cm−1 attributed to the symmetric tensile vibration of SiOSi and the peak of 789 cm−1 corresponding to the vibration of SiO (Fig. 2A-b), proving that SiO2 was successfully coated on the surface of carbon dots. At the same time, the tensile vibration at 3429 cm−1 and the in-plane bending vibration at 1562 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of NH bonds, which proved that APTES was successfully modified on the surface of CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 2A-b). As shown in Fig. 2A-c, the peak at 3429 cm−1 was due to OH tensile vibration, and the peak at 1720 cm−1 was due to CO tensile vibration. It proved that mercaptopropionic acid was successfully modified on the surface of CdTe QDs. The stretching bands of COO– were at 1648 cm−1 and 1400-1, and the OH stretching vibration peak was at 3427 cm−1, which showed the successfully grafting of r-QDs (Fig. 2A-d). The FT-IR spectra of CDs @ SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (Fig. 2A-e) had little change with the CDs @ SiO2-r-QDs (Fig. 2A-d). So the zeta potential and XPS of the nanoparticles also been studied for verifying the composition.

The zeta potential of the nanoparticles was measured to investigate the synthesis procedure. As shown in Fig. 2B, the zeta potentials of CDs and r-QDs were −20.63 ± 0.6 mV (Fig. 2B-a) and −17.4 ± 1.1 mV (Fig. 2B-c), respectively. After CDs were coated with silicon dioxide, the potential of CDs dropped to −6.69 ± 0.51 mV (Fig. 2B-b), which also proved the successful preparation of CDs@SiO2. After the grafting of CDs@SiO2 with r-QDs, the zeta potential became −24.3 ± 1.41 mV (Fig. 2B-d), indicating that r-QDs successfully connected to the surface of CDs@SiO2. In addition, the zeta potential of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab was observed to be lower (−28.4 ± 0.23 mV) (Fig. 2B-e) than that of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs, which indicated that the negatively charged antibody protein has been successfully grafted to the surface of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs. The XPS was also been measured to verify the composition of the probe. As shown in Fig. 2C, Si2p at 101.1 eV, C1S at 284.8 eV, N1s at 400 eV, Cd3d at 403 eV, O1s at 531 eV, and Te3d at 572 eV has strong signals, which further verified the element composition of the probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical propertied of the antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs@SiO2 and CdTe quantum dots were characterized by their fluorescence properties. As can be seen from Fig. 2D, the blue CDs (curve a), CDs@SiO2 (curve b) and the red CdTe QDs (curve c) have maximum fluorescence emission of 500 nm, 500 nm and 606 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectrum (curve d) of the synthetic probe CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab showed that the maximum emission wavelength was around 494 nm and 608 nm under excitation at 400 nm. Under 365 UV lamp, the CDs (the inset of Fig. 2D-a) and CDs@SiO2 (the inset of Fig. 2D-b) showed a blue color, r-QDs (the inset of Fig. 2D-c) showed a red color. At last, the synthetic probe (CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab) (the inset of Fig. 2D-d) showed a pink color.

In order to obtain high sensitivity for the cephalexin detection, some related factors, such as adsorption time, time stability, temperature stability and pH of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab were optimized. In the kinetics experiment, three portions of cephalexin were added to the probe solution. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (FL) (I608/I494) increased rapidly with time increasing within the first 10 min. Then the FL ratio increased slowly, and reached maximum within 30 min. This may be due to the large number of cephalexin antibodies on the surface of the probe, and the specific adsorption accelerates the enrichment of cephalexin in the solution. As time going, the number of cephalexin antibody on the probe decreased and the reaction slowed down. And the fluorescence quenching ratio of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab had not change after 30 min with the adding of cephalexin. In order to make the response completed, 60 min was chosen as the adsorption time. In the other hand, when the first portion of cephalexin was added, the equilibrium was reached. And then, the introduction of additional cephalexin caused the changes of FL ratio and reestablishment of the reaction equilibrium. These results also demonstrated the advantages of ratiometric probe that it can quantitative measurement of analyte concentrations independent of the probe concentration [28,29]. As shown the Fig. 3B, the stability of the as-prepared CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab was systematically investigated within 7 days. The ratio of FL remained no apparent change, implying that the ratiometric fluorescent sensor has good stability and could be applied for cephalexin detection. And CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab was obviously stable in the range of 19–49 °C (Fig. 3C), which also provided a guarantee for better detection of cephalexin. Furthermore, the effect of different pH on sensor was also investigated. The results showed that there were apparent FL change of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab with the pH from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 3D), and the ratios of FL intensity of the CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab solutions basically increased. At pH 10.0, the absorption peak at 608 nm was the largest and the fluorescence intensity was the strongest. Considering that too strong alkaline will affect the activity of antibody protein, so pH 7.4 was chosen as the optimum pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibody-functional ratiometric fluorescent sensor for cephalexin detection
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the FL of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab continuously decreased at 608 nm with the concentration of cephalexin increased. There were a good linear relationship between the CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab and the cephalexin concentration in the range of 1–500 μM. The linear curve fitted an equation (I608/I494)0/(I608/I494) = 0.0013C + 1.0805 (R2 = 0.9913) (C refer to the concentration of cephalexin) (Fig. 4B). The limits of detection (LOD) for cephalexin were 0.7 μM. And the color of the solution gradually changed from pink to blue under 365 nm UV lamps, along with the changes of the ratiometric fluorescence signal (inset of Fig. 4A). This provided an easy detection method with eyes.

The selectivity was a vital parameter which identifies targets in a mixture. To verify the specific detection of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab for cephalexin, we selected several drugs (including cephalexin, amoxicillin, ampicillin, penicillin sodium, chloramphenicol, atenolol), and some other possible substances in milk (including cysteine, glutamic acid, and glucose, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cl-) for experiments. As can be seen in Fig. 4C, the FL ratio of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab has not obvious change on the other substances except for cephalexin. In particular, negligible interference was also observed for detecting cephalexin in the presence of 10-folds of other drugs or possible substances (Fig. 4C). These results inhibited that the antibody-modification endows the probe high selectivity.

To understand the mechanism of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab probe to detect cephalexin, UV spectrum and fluorescent spectra were studied. According to previous report [30], fluorescence quenching is generally divided into dynamic quenching and static quenching. The increasing of the temperature causes the quenching of fluorescence, which ascribed to effect of dynamic quenching. However, the quenching effect of static quenching decreases with increasing of temperature. Therefore, we determined the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab and cephalexin at different temperatures. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab probe was quenched at 608 nm, and the value of I608/I494 nm decreases significantly with the increase of temperature, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching of the probe sensor studied was a dynamic quenching process (Fig. S1). According to the UV spectrum analysis of cephalexin (Fig. 4D-a) and CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab (Fig. 4D-b), the UV absorption of the probe has overlap with the UV absorption band of cephalexin. Meanwhile, there is no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of cephalexin (Fig. 4D-a) and fluorescent emission of the as-prepared sensor (Fig. 4D-c). We can rule out electron transfer as a possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching. So we presumably considered to be the transfer of charges from CDs@SiO2-r-QDs-Ab to cephalexin. The charge in the conduction band of the probe can be transferred to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of cephalexin [31–33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable device with smartphone as analyzer for cephalexin detection in milk
ce:section.ce:para: The application of the above-mentioned fluorescence method requires a fluorescence spectrometer, but the spectrometer was limited in the on-line monitoring of actual samples. In order to solve this problem, we designed a portable device based on a smartphone to replace the fluorescence spectrometer. The detection process was shown in Fig. 5 A. Firstly, pre-treatment of the milk sample were built. The sample was tested with a simple device. The detection device was made of black material to reduce the influence of external light sources. An UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm was fixed at the bottom to provide an excitation light source. Sample stage was installed in the portable device to fix the distance between the sample and the UV lamp to ensure the accuracy of detection. The hole in the front of the device was used to take photos with a smartphone. The color picker APP was used to convert the photo color signal into digital values of the red (R), green (G) and blue (B), so that quantitative analysis can be performed. The home page and detection interface of the APP were shown in Fig. 5B.

The samples containing different concentrations of cephalexin were placed in the sample stage of the portable device which exhibited different fluorescent colors under UV lamp. After taking picture (Fig. 5C), images of different colors are converted to RGB values through Color Picker APP. As shown in Fig. 5D, the ratio of red (R) to blue (B) channels (R/B) depended linearly on the concentration of cephalexin ranging from 1 μM to 500 μM. The linear regression equation was R/B = −9.12 × 10-4C + 0.93 (R2 = 0.96) (C refered to the concentration of cephalexin). This showed that the analysis based on smartphones is feasible for quantitative determination of cephalexin.

Finally, we studied the application of this method for the photosensitive detection of cephalexin in actual samples (ie. milk) (Table 1 ) with the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Four different concentrations of cephalexin (0 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 50 μM) were added to two different milk (whole milk and skimmed milk), and the standard addition method was performed to confirm the analysis results. The recovery rate of this method was from 94.1% to 102.2%, which fully proves that the sensor can selectively and sensitively detect cephalexin in actual samples.

Some previously reported cephalexin fluorescence detection methods [1,34–37] are listed in Table 2 . As shown in Table 2, the sensor we developed has obvious advantages compared with other detection methods. The ratio of fluorescent probe can not only realize laboratory detection through fluorescence spectroscopy, but can also realize online monitoring of real samples through integrated smart phones. The test was completed within 1 min and the results were satisfactory. At the same time, the equipment is simple and portable and does not require specialized personnel to operate.

Dual-emission fluorescent probe combined smartphone based on QDs and antibody-modification was successfully prepared. CDs coated with silica spacer were used as reference probe. Red-emission CdTe QDs modified with antibody were used as response probe. The results showed that the as-prepared probe can selectively and sensitively detect cephalexin with the linear range of 1–500 μM and the LOD of 0.7 μM. When the probe was applied to the detection of milk, the recovery was from 94.1% to 102.2%. Moreover, this strategy integrated smartphone can also realize the visual detection of cephalexin, which provides a simple and portable detection of cephalexin in the milk at low cost. The method has good prospects in the detection of other antibiotic residues in animal-derived foods and agricultural products.

Ai-Yue Hao: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Software. Xue-Qing Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Resources. Yan-Zhen Mei: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jun-Fang Nie: Methodology, Investigation. Ya-Qiong Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Chuan-Chao Dai: Conceptualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Technologies Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC1604206), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21904064), Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB150028).
